<!-- question-type: prepare -->
### Exercise 1: The What and Why of employee attrition

Employee attrition refers to people leaving the company — either voluntarily (resignation) or involuntarily (termination). 
While some turnover is normal, high attrition can signal deeper issues.

**(a)** What kinds of consequences might high attrition have for a business?

**(b)** What might cause employees to leave?

**(c)** Why might an analytics team want to group employees into segments when studying attrition, rather than looking only at employees one by one?

<!-- BEGIN PROFILE:r-teaching-guide -->

::: {.content-visible when-profile="r-teaching-guide"}

::: {.teaching-block}

::: {.teaching-block-header}
Teaching Note
:::

::: {.teaching-block-body}

🎯 **Learning Objective**

Students should:

* Understand the concept of employee attrition and its business implications.
* Reflect on possible drivers of employee turnover.
* Recognize why grouping employees into segments may help an analytics team better understand attrition patterns.

✅   **Core Concepts to Highlight**

* Definition of employee attrition
* Business impacts of high attrition, including costs, disruption, morale, and loss of firm-specific knowledge
* Common causes of attrition, such as dissatisfaction, limited advancement, management fit, pay, or commute burden
* Segmentation as a descriptive analytics tool for identifying broader employee profiles

💬 **Suggested In-Class Prompts** (if needed)

* “What kinds of costs might a firm face when experienced employees leave?”
* “Could two employees both leave for very different reasons?”
* “Why might an analytics team learn more from studying groups of employees rather than only individual cases?”

📌 **Common Misunderstandings**

* Assuming all attrition is bad, when some turnover is normal or even healthy
* Focusing only on pay as a cause of attrition and ignoring workplace or personal context
* Thinking segmentation is only a marketing idea rather than a broader analytics tool
* Jumping straight to prediction, when this tutorial is first about understanding patterns

:::

:::

:::

<!-- END PROFILE:r-teaching-guide -->

<!-- BEGIN PROFILE:r-solutions -->

::: {.content-visible when-profile="r-solutions" when-profile="r-teaching-guide"}

::: {.solution-block}

::: {.solution-block-header}
Solution
:::

::: {.solution-block-body}

**(a) What kinds of consequences might high attrition have for a business?**

* **Increased costs**: Hiring and training new employees takes time and money.
* **Loss of knowledge**: Experienced employees may take valuable firm-specific knowledge with them.
* **Disruption to teams**: Turnover can interrupt workflows and reduce team stability.
* **Reduced productivity**: New hires may need time to become fully effective.
* **Morale and reputation effects**: High attrition can lower morale and may signal a poor work environment.

**(b) What are some possible reasons employees might leave?**

* **Job dissatisfaction**: Employees may be unhappy with their role, workload, manager, or workplace culture.
* **Limited growth opportunities**: Some employees may leave if they feel stuck or underutilized.
* **Compensation and benefits**: Employees may move for better pay or conditions elsewhere.
* **Commute or location issues**: Long travel times may make a job less sustainable.
* **Personal reasons**: Family needs, health, or career changes may also matter.

**(c) Why might an analytics team want to group employees into segments when studying attrition, rather than looking only at employees one by one?**

* **Pattern finding**: Segments can reveal broader employee profiles with different attrition patterns.
* **Clearer interpretation**: Grouping can make it easier to describe which types of employees are more likely to leave.
* **Better actionability**: Firms may be able to design different retention responses for different employee groups.
* **Descriptive insight before prediction**: Segmentation helps analysts understand the structure of the workforce before moving to more formal predictive tools.

:::

:::

:::

<!-- END PROFILE:r-solutions -->
